title: Add --gpx <path> flag to plan command for OsmAnd/Locus/Gaia export
description:
Emit GPX from plan itineraries so they can be loaded into OsmAnd (Android) and other GPX-consuming mapping apps (Locus Map, Gaia GPS, Organic Maps, Mapy.cz, Komoot upload).

## Shape

- New flag: `--gpx <path>` on `ptv plan`, parallel to existing `--html <path>`.
- New module: `src/plan/gpx.ts` exporting `writeGpx(itineraries, path)`, mirroring `map.ts` structure.
- Each labeled itinerary → one `<trk>` with `<name>` = label (e.g. 'fastest', 'recommended', 'max-path').
- One `<trkseg>` per leg:
  - Bike legs: use the decoded polyline geometry already on `BikeLeg.geometry` (lat/lon points).
  - Train legs: straight-line segment between origin and destination station coords (matches current map.ts behavior).
- Include `<wpt>` markers for transfer points (station boardings/alightings) with stop name.
- Wire into `src/commands/plan.ts` after the html branch; call `open <path>` afterward (matches the `--html` UX).

## Delivery to phone (out of scope, just docs)

- Add a short README/CLAUDE.md note describing the recommended push paths: Syncthing folder watched by OsmAnd, Tailscale Drop, or ad-hoc `python3 -m http.server` + QR.

## Tests

- Unit: `tests/unit/plan/gpx.test.ts` — given a synthetic itinerary, assert XML structure (well-formed, correct `<trk>`/`<trkseg>`/`<trkpt lat lon>` counts, label names).
- E2e: extend `tests/e2e/plan.test.ts` with a case that runs `plan ... --gpx /tmp/x.gpx` and asserts the file exists + parses as XML.

## Tradeoff to note

GPX is a static snapshot — no live re-routing if you deviate. For dynamic routing on the phone the user would need OsmAnd's own routing or a self-hosted GraphHopper/Valhalla — explicitly out of scope here.

## Related

- See `src/plan/map.ts` as the structural template.
- Bike geometry source: `BikeLeg.geometry` (already populated by osrm-au decode in `external.ts`).

title: v1.7: color map polylines by hilliness
description:
Render bike-leg polylines on the Leaflet map (src/plan/map.ts) colored by per-segment grade rather than a single solid green. Lets users visually identify the steep sections of a ride at a glance, without clicking each leg for the popup.

Today every bike polyline is uniformly #2a7 (green). After v1.5 we have per-segment slope data via gh-route's details.average_slope. Combining the GeoJSON coordinates with the slope segments gives us color per visible polyline span.
design:
Two implementation paths:

**Option A (simpler): segment the polyline at slope boundaries**
For each bike leg, walk gh-route's details.average_slope = [[from_idx, to_idx, grade%], ...]. For each (from_idx, to_idx) span, slice the geometry.coordinates accordingly and emit a separate L.polyline with color chosen by grade. ~30-40 LOC in map.ts.

Color scheme (proposed):
  grade <= -6%   : #1976d2 (steep down — blue)
  -6 < grade <= -2%: #4fc3f7 (gentle down — light blue)
  -2 < grade <  2% : #4caf50 (flat — green)
  2 <= grade <  4%: #ffc107 (gentle up — amber)
  4 <= grade <  6%: #ff9800 (moderate up — orange)
  6 <= grade <  8%: #f44336 (steep up — red)
  grade >= 8%      : #b71c1c (very steep up — dark red)

**Option B (more elegant): use a GeoJSON FeatureCollection with per-feature styling**
Replace the single L.polyline call with L.geoJSON(...) and style each feature by its grade property. Cleaner abstraction; same visual result.

Path data flow:
- The bike leg already carries 'geometry: GeoJsonLineString' and the per-segment slope is in the underlying parseGhRoute response — but we don't currently propagate average_slope onto BikeLeg.
- Need to add 'averageSlope?: Array<[number, number, number]>' to BikeLeg in types.ts, populate it in parseGhRoute's return, thread through orchestrator and planBikeOnly.
- Then map.ts can read it.

Legend: add a small color-coded gradient strip in the existing Leaflet legend control (bottom-right), showing the grade ranges. ~10 LOC.

Touches: src/plan/types.ts (BikeLeg field), src/plan/external.ts (return averageSlope from parseGhRoute), src/plan/orchestrator.ts (thread through both K=1, K=2, planBikeOnly), src/plan/map.ts (multi-segment polyline rendering + legend).

Estimated ~100 LOC + tests.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Bike-leg polylines split into segments colored by grade%
- [ ] Map legend shows the color → grade mapping
- [ ] Bike-leg popup still shows km/min/%-on-path (no regression)
- [ ] Falls back to solid green when averageSlope data is absent (commute mode without --enrich)
- [ ] Unit test for the polyline-splitting helper
- [ ] Existing e2e map test still passes (HTML contains leaflet@1.9.4)

title: plan: add --google comparison engine (Routes API)
description:
Add a --google flag to ptv plan that calls Google Routes API (routes.googleapis.com:computeRoutes, travelMode=BICYCLE, computeAlternativeRoutes=true) and returns its alternatives alongside ours for benchmarking.

Reads GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY from env (no flag); skip silently if absent.
Decode encoded polyline, attach as a BikeLeg-shaped object with label 'google:<description>' (e.g. 'google:Plenty River Trail').
Surface in JSON output and HTML overlay so we can visually diff geometry vs osrm-au / gh-route.
Field mask: routes.distanceMeters,routes.duration,routes.description,routes.polyline.encodedPolyline,routes.legs.steps.

Motivation: Rosanna→Eltham probe (2026-05-17) showed Google picks Plenty River Trail spine while ours uses Diamond Creek Trail spine. Both are 59min but the geometries differ by up to 2km — useful to have Google as a permanent benchmark for tuning the day-ride custom model.

title: v1.5: --goal flag for commute vs day-ride scoring presets
description: User's 'two use cases' framing 2026-05-17: getting somewhere (min time) vs going for a ride (max time on cycleway/path, accept extra distance). Currently --prefer-bike-path biases toward higher bikeKmOnPath but uses a fixed PATH_BONUS_PER_KM=5 constant. The two use cases have genuinely different cost functions — one preset can't cover both well.
design:
Add --goal <commute|day-ride|auto> flag, default 'auto' (current behavior = commute-like).

Commute preset (current default):
  - recommended = min totalTimeMin
  - PATH_BONUS_PER_KM = 0 effectively unless --prefer-bike-path

Day-ride preset:
  - recommended = max bikeKmOnPath
  - hard cap: distance ≤ 2x shortest-path option
  - hard cap: maxSustainedGradePercent × maxSustainedGradeM ≤ TBD threshold (e.g. 3000)
  - Suggests adjacent-line termini queries: 'a ride to a different train line'

Implementation:
  - Add goal: 'commute' | 'day-ride' | 'auto' to PlanRequest
  - score.ts branches on goal for cost function
  - Add validation: --goal day-ride incompatible with --max-bike-km < shortest+50%
  - Document the presets in CLAUDE.md

Estimated ~50 LOC.

Related: ptv-78j (elevation), ptv-dzc (min-path-fraction), ptv-m7o (bike-only mode). Day-ride goal naturally composes with min-path-fraction.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `ptv plan A B --goal day-ride` returns recommended itinerary with highest bikeKmOnPath
- [ ] Day-ride mode rejects itineraries with km > 2x shortest
- [ ] Day-ride mode rejects itineraries with max-sustained-climb-intensity above threshold
- [ ] --goal auto behaves identically to v1.4 default
- [ ] Unit test per preset
- [ ] E2e suite stays green

title: v2.0: bike popularity overlay (Strava heatmap or similar)
description:
User idea 2026-05-17: 'go places where other people ride.'

Use ride-popularity data to prefer well-traveled bike routes. Other riders' choices encode local knowledge (which paths are nicer, which roads are tolerable despite gh-route classification) that algorithmic profiles miss.
design:
Sources to evaluate:
  - Strava Metro (paid, license needed) — best heatmap of actual rides
  - Strava heatmap tiles (public, rate-limited)
  - OSM 'bicycle=designated' tag density as a proxy
  - Local council 'principal bike network' shapefiles (Melbourne)

Integration: load popularity data as a per-edge multiplier in the GraphHopper custom_model, OR as a post-hoc scoring overlay (score each candidate route by % of distance that falls inside a popularity-weighted corridor).

Practical approach: start with the post-hoc scoring overlay. Less invasive, doesn't need a custom GraphHopper model. Load a static GeoJSON of 'popular bike corridors' (manually curated for Melbourne to start), buffer each by 100m, compute % of route inside any corridor.

External data sources to investigate:
  - bicyclenetwork.com.au Melbourne maps
  - VicRoads Strategic Cycling Corridor data
  - data.gov.au public bike-related datasets

Bigger scope, depends on data sourcing. Probably v2.0 or later.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Identify a free/usable popularity data source for Melbourne
- [ ] popularityFraction or similar metric appears in BikeLeg output
- [ ] Optional --prefer-popular flag biases recommended toward higher popularity score

title: feat: web /api/plan — parse depart/arrive-by in PlanBody using parseMelbourneHHMM
v1.5: surface elevation data from gh-route and add prefer-flat scoring
description:
gh-route returns ascend (m) and descend (m) on every path, plus per-segment slope buckets in details.average_slope. We currently discard all of this. User feedback (2026-05-17): 'the fastest route is much slower because of big hills' — bike profile's time estimate underweights elevation effort, so a hilly 'fastest' route is misleading.

Probe data, same Rosanna→Heidelberg query (2.3 km direct):
  bike            ascend=52m
  bike_quiet      ascend=47m  (+0.44 km distance, only 5m saved climb)
  bike_balanced   ascend=51m

For longer trips this delta would grow. Elevation matters but is invisible in current output.
design:
Two additive changes, both in the existing parseGhRoute path:

1. **Surface ascend/descend on BikeLeg**:
   - Add ascendM?: number, descendM?: number to BikeLeg in types.ts
   - parseGhRoute in external.ts extracts p.ascend / p.descend and returns them
   - ghRouteBike's return type gains ascendM/descendM
   - orchestrator threads them into BikeLeg
   - JSON output: bike legs gain ascendM/descendM fields; aggregate at itinerary level (sum across all bike legs into ascendM/descendM on Itinerary)

2. **--prefer-flat flag with weighted cost** (mirrors --prefer-bike-path):
   - Add preferFlat: boolean to PlanRequest
   - When true, biased cost: totalTimeMin + ASCEND_PENALTY_PER_M * totalAscendM
   - Constant ASCEND_PENALTY_PER_M = 0.05 (3 minutes per 60m of climbing, roughly matching real human bike speed loss on 5% grade)
   - 'recommended' label uses biased cost
   - Falls back gracefully when no itinerary has ascendM populated

Touches: src/plan/external.ts (parser), src/plan/types.ts (BikeLeg, Itinerary, PlanRequest, constant), src/plan/orchestrator.ts (aggregate ascend across bike legs into itinerary), src/plan/score.ts (cost branching), src/commands/plan.ts (flag).

Estimated ~80 LOC + tests.

Stretch (defer to v1.6 if scope creeps): --max-ascend-m <n> hard filter, --avoid-steep flag that penalizes segments with slope > X.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] BikeLeg JSON includes ascendM and descendM (numbers in meters) when --enrich is on
- [ ] Itinerary JSON includes aggregate ascendM and descendM
- [ ] `--prefer-flat` flag biases 'recommended' toward lower-ascent itineraries
- [ ] Combined with --prefer-bike-path: both biases apply (cost = time - path_bonus*kmOnPath + ascend_penalty*ascendM)
- [ ] Falls back gracefully when no enrichment data
- [ ] Unit tests: parser captures ascend, aggregation correct, biased cost picks flatter option
- [ ] E2e: full suite stays green
notes:
**Important refinement from Rosanna→Heidelberg analysis 2026-05-17:**

Total ascend is NOT the right metric. All three profiles climbed 47-52m for this corridor — within 10% of each other — yet they FEEL completely different. The user's perception ('bike_quiet is MUCH nicer ride, until it turns the corner to go up Darebin street') is captured by the slope DISTRIBUTION, not the sum.

Real signal lives in details.average_slope, which gives per-segment grade %. Specifically:

| metric | bike | bike_quiet | bike_balanced |
|---|---|---|---|
| % distance at ≤4% grade | 34% | **74%** | 37% |
| % distance at ≥6% grade | 23% | 15% | 25% |
| worst single climb | 7%×216m | **8%×191m** ← Darebin | 7%×216m |
| worst single descent | -9%×143m | **-12%×100m** | -9%×143m |

So bike_quiet is genuinely flatter-on-average but has the single worst climb (the 'Darebin Street' moment). User's local knowledge matched the data exactly.

**Revised metrics to surface and score on:**

Per BikeLeg, add:
  - ascendM, descendM (existing plan)
  - **maxSustainedGradePercent** — the worst single climb-run's grade
  - **maxSustainedGradeM** — distance of that climb
  - **flatFraction** — share of bike distance at |grade| ≤ 4%
  - **steepFraction** — share at |grade| ≥ 6%

A 'comfort score' could combine: penalize maxSustainedGradePercent * maxSustainedGradeM AND penalize 1 - flatFraction. Tuning TBD.

Replace the simple ASCEND_PENALTY_PER_M with two:
  - SUSTAINED_CLIMB_PENALTY_PER_M_AT_PERCENT (e.g. 0.02 min per metre at percent — so 200m at 8% = 32 'penalty minutes')
  - FLAT_FRACTION_BONUS_PER_PERCENT (positive bonus for high flatFraction)

This is materially more code than the original bead — closer to ~150 LOC + tests. Consider promoting to its own spec rather than bundling into v1.5 alongside everything else.

title: fix: web /api/plan — migrate osrm-au subprocess to LAN OSRM REST for container deploy
close_reason: Migrated osrm-au subprocess to LAN OSRM REST. /api/plan --goal commute now works in-container. Spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-18-osrm-rest-migration-design.md, plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-18-osrm-rest-migration.md, commits 2737cb3 + 2b3e661 + c4876e2 + fd42a48.

title: feat: web /api/plan — log per-request plan params, cache hit/miss, duration (PRD N5)
v2.0: path-stringing route algorithm for nice-ride mode
description:
User idea 2026-05-17: 'another way to make rides is to join up all the paths / quiet roads.'

Distinct from --prefer-bike-path (post-hoc scoring) and --goal day-ride (preset). This is a route GENERATION change: bias the underlying graph search toward edges of class cycleway/path/track, even at substantial distance cost. Currently gh-route's bike_quiet picks low-stress residential streets but doesn't aggressively connect bike-path fragments.
design:
Approach: use GraphHopper's custom_model feature server-side, or post-process via constrained shortest path.

Custom model JSON: priority multiplied by 5-10x on cycleway/path/track classes; small penalty on residential; large penalty on tertiary+. Distance gradient can be tuned: typical bike profile has distance_influence ~70, custom path-stringing might use ~30 (more distance-tolerant).

Calls would look like:
  curl -X POST http://graphhopper.../route -d '{
    "points": [[lat,lon],[lat,lon]],
    "profile": "bike",
    "ch.disable": true,
    "custom_model": {
      "priority": [
        {"if": "road_class == CYCLEWAY", "multiply_by": 5.0},
        {"if": "road_class == PATH", "multiply_by": 5.0},
        {"if": "road_class == TRACK", "multiply_by": 4.0},
        {"if": "road_class == TERTIARY", "multiply_by": 0.3}
      ],
      "distance_influence": 30
    }
  }'

Tasks:
  - Extend gh-route CLI to accept --custom-model FILE (or build it ourselves via direct GraphHopper REST)
  - Add --goal nicest-ride preset using this custom model
  - Test on Lilydale→Hurstbridge (current bike_quiet finds 52% on path; goal: >75%)

Larger scope, v2.0 territory. Depends on either gh-route extension or direct GraphHopper integration.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Custom-model request from ptv finds path-heavy routes that bike_quiet misses
- [ ] Validation: tested on Lilydale→Hurstbridge, Lilydale→Belgrave with measurable improvement in bikeKmOnPath
- [ ] Falls back to bike_quiet if GraphHopper rejects the custom model
- [ ] Tests for the request shape and response parsing
notes:
Probe completed 2026-05-17 — confirmed dramatic improvement over gh-route's three preset profiles.

Lilydale → Hurstbridge (the user's example corridor):

| option                  | km   | path-km | busy-km | flat% | hardest    |
|-------------------------|------|---------|---------|-------|------------|
| bike (default)          | 31.5 | 1.2     | 17.4    | 55%   | 12%×877m   |
| bike_quiet              | 56.3 | 24.6    | 2.3     | 74%   | 12%×475m   |
| bike_balanced           | 48.2 | 14.2    | 9.0     | 62%   | 16%×562m   |
| **penalize-busy**       | 44.7 | 27.6    | 0.6     | 73%   | 16%×394m   |
| **strong-prefer-path**  | 51.9 | 46.2    | 0.0     | 86%   | 7%×339m    |
| **max-path-aggressive** | 52.1 | 46.8    | 0.0     | 86%   | 7%×339m    |

strong-prefer-path beats bike_quiet on EVERY dimension:
- Slightly shorter (52 vs 56 km)
- 87% more path (46 vs 25 km)
- Zero busy roads (vs 2.3 km)
- Less steep (7%x339m vs 12%x475m)
- More flat (86% vs 74%)

The magic ingredient was a 0.7 multiplier on RESIDENTIAL — forces search OFF residential streets ONTO adjacent cycleway. distance_influence=50 (vs default 70) lets the algorithm accept reasonable detours.

GraphHopper REST custom_model API works as documented; ch.disable=true required. POST to http://graphhopper.magpie-inconnu.ts.net:8989/route with:
  points, profile=bike, ch.disable=true, points_encoded=false,
  details: ['road_class','average_slope','surface'],
  custom_model: { priority: [...], distance_influence: N }

IMPORTANT: custom_model multiply_by must be in (0, 1]. Cannot boost classes above default; can only penalize others. Pattern is 'penalize roads', not 'boost cycleway'.

Implementation path forward:
1. Extend src/plan/external.ts with ghRouteCustom(from, to, customModel)
2. Add three presets: 'commute' (default), 'nice-ride' (strong-prefer-path), 'max-path' (aggressive)
3. Wire to --goal flag from ptv-53s: --goal day-ride uses nice-ride; --goal max-path uses aggressive
4. Tests with mocked fetch responses

Expected ~120 LOC + tests. Combines well with ptv-53s (goal flag) and ptv-dzc (min-path-fraction).

title: plan: kmOnPath uses coordinate-index span instead of metres (~6% inflation)
description:
In src/plan/external.ts:195, kmOnPath is computed as:

    kmOnPath = km * (pathIdx / totalRcIdx)

where pathIdx/totalRcIdx sum *coordinate-index spans* from GraphHopper's road_class detail (each entry is [fromIdx, toIdx, classname]). This implicitly assumes every coordinate-index represents equal real-world distance, which is false — denser polyline points (e.g. wigglier cycleway geometry) inflate the path fraction.

Measured on Rosanna→Eltham day-ride (2026-05-17):
- reported bikeKmOnPath: 13.5 km (84%)
- actual cycleway/path/track metres (per-segment haversine): 12.5 km (77%)
- ~6% inflation

Fix: compute per-segment haversine metres once (we already do this for slope analysis in the same function), then sum metres rather than index spans when summing PATH_ROAD_CLASSES vs total. Reuse segDistM[] from the slope block.

Acceptance: add a unit test with a synthetic raw response where path edges have artificially dense points to prove metric is metre-based not index-based.

title: feat: web results card — add on-path %, ascend, wait + segment-bar render
v1.5: --min-on-path-fraction soft constraint for bike-path coverage
description:
Soft constraint that filters itineraries to those where at least F of bike distance is on dedicated cycling infrastructure (cycleway/path/track).

Surfaced 2026-05-17 during Rosanna→Heidelberg path-profile comparison. Today --prefer-bike-path biases the 'recommended' label toward higher bikeKmOnPath itineraries, but offers no way to require a minimum. For 2 km corridors with no nearby trails, all three gh-route profiles returned 0-25% on path; user has no way to express 'don't show me anything under 50% on dedicated paths'.
design:
Add --min-on-path-fraction <f> flag on ptv plan, value in [0, 1].

Semantics:
  - Hard filter on bikeKmOnPath / bikeKm < f → drop itinerary
  - If all itineraries fail, return near-miss with constraintsViolated: ['min_on_path_fraction'] (mirrors min_bike_km near-miss path)
  - Only meaningful when --enrich is on (gh-route populates bikeKmOnPath); error if --no-enrich combined with this flag
  - Validate f is in [0, 1]; reject negative or > 1

Touches: src/plan/score.ts (filter + near-miss), src/plan/types.ts (PlanRequest.minOnPathFraction, ConstraintViolation extension), src/commands/plan.ts (flag + validation).

Estimated ~40 LOC + tests.

Related: ptv-m7o (--mode bike-only). Could compose: --mode bike-only --min-on-path-fraction 0.5 = 'show me a bike-only route that's mostly on paths'.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `ptv plan A B --min-on-path-fraction 0.5` filters out itineraries under 50% on-path
- [ ] All-filtered case returns near-miss with constraintsViolated populated
- [ ] Combined with --no-enrich: exits non-zero
- [ ] f outside [0,1]: exits non-zero
- [ ] Unit test: filter behavior and near-miss path
- [ ] E2e: full suite stays green

title: v1.5: --mode bike-only for direct bike trips
description:
Pure-bike mode so short same-suburb trips (e.g. Rosanna→Heidelberg, 2.34 km) can be planned without the bike+train+bike algorithm shape forcing a train leg.

Surfaced 2026-05-17 when Doctor Dee asked for 'a bike trip from Rosanna to Heidelberg'. The v1.4 planner always inserts a train leg, so we had to hand-build via a Node one-liner calling osrm-au directly and feeding a fabricated PlanResult into writeMapHtml. Workaround works but isn't a CLI experience users can reach for.
design:
Add --mode <auto|bike-only|bike-train> flag on ptv plan, default 'auto' (current behavior).

In bike-only mode:
  - skip K=1/K=2 search entirely
  - call osrmRoute('bicycle', from, to) once
  - emit a 1-leg PlanResult { transfers: 0, legs: [bike] }
  - labels: ['recommended', 'fastest']
  - constraint check: reject --mode bike-only combined with --max-transfers > 0 (with a clear stderr error)

Touches: src/plan/orchestrator.ts (early-return branch), src/commands/plan.ts (flag + PlanRequest field), src/plan/types.ts (PlanRequest.mode field).

Estimated ~30 LOC + tests. Group with other v1.5 candidates: full train-pattern polylines (deferred from v1.4), K=3 transfers (deferred from v1.2), --bike-profile bike_quiet|bike_balanced (deferred from v1.1).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `ptv plan A B --mode bike-only --html trip.html` returns a 1-leg itinerary and writes a usable map
- [ ] `ptv plan A B --mode bike-only --max-transfers 1` exits non-zero with clear error
- [ ] Default behavior (no --mode) unchanged
- [ ] Unit test: bike-only mode returns 1 leg with transfers=0, bike leg geometry populated
- [ ] E2e: full suite stays green

title: fix: web cache — escape ':' in ns/k to remove theoretical key collision
description: Cache.key(ns, k) builds 'ptv:${ns}:${k}'. If ns or k ever contains ':', two distinct (ns,k) pairs can collide. Current callers are internal so it's theoretical, but an encodeURIComponent on segments would close the door.

title: test: web — add unit test for render.ts template engine
description: render.ts (the dependency-free template engine) is currently exercised only via integration tests. Add a dedicated unit test covering: {{var}} escaping, {{{var}}} raw, {{#each items}} loops, dotted-path access, empty-array #each, missing-key fallback.

title: Web frontend for ptv plan (epic)
description:
Build a web UI for the `ptv plan` command so it can be used from a phone/browser without the CLI.

## Strategy

Two-phase build. Phase 1 ships the smallest viable UI; phase 2 adds the map-first UX once the API surface is stable.

Hosting target: **totoro** behind Tailscale (magic-DNS hostname per RMAI infra conventions). This skips auth, PTV credential exposure, and rate-limiting concerns since access is already gated by the tailnet.

## Phases

1. **Phase 1 — HTMX + server-rendered fragments** (see child bead): smallest functional UI. Form + results table + embedded Leaflet map. Server shells/extracts the existing orchestrator.
2. **Phase 2 — Click-to-route Leaflet PWA** (see child bead): map-first UX, geolocation, installable to phone home screen.

## Out of scope (defer)

- Public/internet hosting (would need auth + PTV cred proxy + per-IP limits)
- React/Svelte SPA option — HTMX gets ~80% of the value without a build chain
- Other PTV subcommands (departures, disruptions) — plan is the only one worth a UI

## Architecture decisions to lock in

- Extract `src/plan/orchestrator.ts` behind an HTTP handler in a new `src/server/` module — do **not** shell out to the CLI binary (avoids per-request subprocess cost for the orchestrator itself; osrm-au/gh-route subprocesses still happen but are unavoidable).
- Cache plan results by hash of `(from, to, depart/arrive, goal, mode, ...all-flags)` — biggest single perf win.
- Reuse `src/plan/map.ts` Leaflet rendering as the map layer in both phases.

title: Phase 1: HTMX web UI for ptv plan, tailnet-hosted
description:
Smallest viable web UI for `ptv plan`. Server-rendered HTML + HTMX fragment swaps, no SPA build chain.

## Shape

- New module: `src/server/` with Fastify (or Express — pick whichever has lighter dep weight).
- New entrypoint: `ptv serve [--port 8080]` registered as a subcommand in `src/index.ts`.
- Extract `plan/orchestrator.ts` behind `POST /api/plan` — call it directly, don't shell out.
- Routes:
  - `GET /` — full HTML page: form (from/to/depart/goal/mode/etc.), empty results div, empty map div.
  - `POST /plan` — runs plan(), returns HTML fragment (results table + Leaflet map init script) that HTMX swaps into the page.
  - `GET /static/*` — Leaflet CSS/JS (vendored, no CDN — works on tailnet without external network).
- Reuse `src/plan/map.ts` for the Leaflet rendering — refactor to expose a 'render to existing div' variant alongside the existing 'write full HTML file' variant.

## Form fields

Mirror the CLI flags exactly: from, to, depart OR arrive-by, min/max bike km, max transfers, mode, goal, prefer-bike-path, hill-weight, min-on-path-fraction. Same defaults.

## Caching

In-memory `Map<hash, Itinerary[]>` keyed by a hash of all plan params. TTL ~10 min (PTV departures go stale). Hash uses `crypto.createHash('sha1')` on JSON.stringify of the normalized request.

## Deployment

- Run on totoro behind Tailscale at `ptv.magpie-inconnu.ts.net:<port>` (or under the existing magpie-inconnu service pattern).
- systemd unit or pm2 — match whatever else on totoro uses.
- See `~/.claude/skills/linux-servers/SKILL.md` for totoro conventions before deploying.

## Tests

- Unit: handler tests with a mocked `plan()` — assert correct fragment HTML for happy path + error path.
- Integration: spin up the server in a test, fire a real `POST /plan` with stubbed PTV/osrm/gh-route, assert response shape.
- E2e: use the playwright-skill skill to drive the form end-to-end against a running dev server.

## Out of scope

- Auth (tailnet-gated)
- URL-shareable trip state (Phase 2 with query-string serialization)
- Click-to-route on map (Phase 2)
- Mobile-PWA install (Phase 2)

title: Phase 2: Click-to-route Leaflet PWA upgrade
description:
Map-first UX layered on top of the Phase 1 server. Installable PWA, geolocation, click two points to plan.

Blocked by ptv-t3x.1 — needs the `POST /api/plan` JSON endpoint to exist (currently Phase 1 returns HTML fragments; this phase consumes JSON instead).

## Shape

- Promote `/api/plan` to return JSON in addition to HTML (content negotiation on `Accept` header, or split into `/api/plan.json` and `/api/plan.html`).
- Map is now the primary UI element, not a result panel.
  - Click point 1 → drops 'from' pin.
  - Click point 2 → drops 'to' pin, auto-fires plan().
  - Itineraries render as toggleable layers (already what `map.ts` does).
  - Params panel collapses/slides over the map.
- `navigator.geolocation` button: 'plan from current location'.
- URL state: `#from=lat,lon&to=lat,lon&goal=max-path&...` — shareable links, browser back/forward works.
- PWA bits:
  - `manifest.webmanifest` with name/icons/theme.
  - Service worker caches app shell + Leaflet assets for offline shell load.
  - IndexedDB cache of the last N planned trips (replay without network).

## Tradeoffs

- Click-to-route is mobile-friendly but requires reverse-geocoding the pins to street names — use Nominatim on tailnet if available, fall back to raw coords.
- Service worker adds complexity; if scope creeps, ship without it and let the browser do its normal HTTP cache.

## Out of scope (still)

- Live re-routing if user deviates from a planned trip (would need OsmAnd or device-side routing engine — see ptv-0dm for the GPX export path)
- Trip history sync across devices

title: v1.6: --goal max-path preset using distance_influence=10
description:
Add a third --goal preset 'max-path' that uses a more aggressive GraphHopper custom_model than 'day-ride'. Empirically validated 2026-05-17 on Hurstbridge → Darebin:

  day-ride (current, di=50): 32.2 km / 117 min / 87% on path / 27.9 km on path / 3.7 km residential
  max-path (di=10, resi=0.3): 36.5 km / 137 min / **98% on path / 35.9 km on path / 0 residential**

Doctor Dee's observation: 'You could route via the Darebin Creek + Main Yarra + Diamond Creek trails and go much farther on trails alone. We want the best ride, not the shortest distance.'

day-ride is well-tuned for sparse trail networks (Lilydale → Hurstbridge, 89% path baseline). max-path is for dense-trail corridors where the algorithm should detour aggressively.
design:
Add to DAY_RIDE_CUSTOM_MODEL alongside a new MAX_PATH_CUSTOM_MODEL constant:

export const MAX_PATH_CUSTOM_MODEL: CustomModel = {
  priority: [
    { if: 'road_class == SECONDARY',   multiply_by: 0.02 },
    { if: 'road_class == PRIMARY',     multiply_by: 0.01 },
    { if: 'road_class == TRUNK',       multiply_by: 0.01 },
    { if: 'road_class == TERTIARY',    multiply_by: 0.1  },
    { if: 'road_class == RESIDENTIAL', multiply_by: 0.3  },
  ],
  distance_influence: 10,
};

PlanGoal extends to 'commute' | 'day-ride' | 'max-path'.
Orchestrator's bikeRouteCall dispatch picks the right constant.
CLI validation accepts the new value.

Estimated ~30 LOC + tests. Trivial extension of v1.5 Task 2's dispatch.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] ptv plan A B --goal max-path uses distance_influence=10 in the custom_model body
- [ ] Hurstbridge → Darebin with --goal max-path returns >=95% on path (live integration test)
- [ ] Existing --goal day-ride behavior unchanged
- [ ] Unit test for the third preset constant
- [ ] E2e test for --goal max-path exits 0
close_reason: Implemented in commit 6705db0. MAX_PATH_CUSTOM_MODEL added to types.ts, both bikeRouteCall and planBikeOnly dispatch sites updated, CLI validation covers max-path, 89 unit tests pass.

title: plan: add --via <lat,lon> waypoint support (intermediate routing)
description:
Add --via <lat,lon> (repeatable) to ptv plan for intermediate waypoint routing.

For our gh-route engine: split into N+1 legs at waypoints, route each, concatenate geometry + sum metrics. Validate each leg with the same constraints (max-bike-km applies per leg or total? — decide).
For Google (when ptv-20a lands): pass through as Routes API 'intermediates'.
For osrm-au: same split-and-concat as gh-route.

Use case: user knows a preferred crossing/landmark that the optimiser doesn't pick (e.g. Candlebark Park footbridge crossing the Yarra, avoiding Fitzsimons Lane bridge). The Rosanna→Eltham→Candlebark probe (2026-05-17) confirmed our engine finds the desired Pauline Toner-style crossing when given the waypoint — but currently requires two CLI invocations + manual concatenation.

title: plan: add google-style day-ride preset (softer SECONDARY, higher distance_influence)
description:
Add a 3rd --goal value (or sub-preset of day-ride) that mimics Google's bike routing tradeoffs — willing to use short busy-road connectors when they shorten the trip significantly.

Sketch (from Rosanna→Eltham analysis, 2026-05-17):
- road_class == SECONDARY: 0.35 (currently 0.1 — too aggressive, forces 1.6km detour to avoid 1.2km of B-road)
- road_class == TERTIARY: 0.6 (currently 0.4)
- road_class == RESIDENTIAL: 0.85 (currently 0.7)
- distance_influence: 100 (currently 50 — weight total distance more)

Target: produce a route within ~200m of Google's Plenty River Trail line on the Rosanna→Eltham probe. Acceptance: write a regression test comparing geometry overlap against Google output (depends on ptv-20a).
